data.title: Bright Horizons targets $3.1B revenue and $5.10 EPS for 2026 while advancing portfolio rationalization
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Bright Horizons Family Solutions Inc. (BFAM) Q4 2025

Management View

  • CEO Stephen Kramer reported "a strong finish to 2025, closing out a year of solid growth and continued progress across the business." Kramer highlighted that "revenue increased 9% to $734 million and adjusted EPS increased 17% to $1.15 both ahead of our expectations." For the full year, revenue was $2.93 billion and adjusted EPS of $4.55, which Kramer said "exceeded the expectations shared at the beginning of the year."
  • Kramer detailed segment performance, stating "back-up care again delivered strong growth and earnings contribution in Q4" with revenue up 17% to $183 million. He noted that existing clients saw double-digit growth in backup users with "population remain[ing] relatively flat, meaning growth was driven by deeper penetration into the eligible population."
  • Turning to full service, Kramer said "revenue increased 6% in the fourth quarter to $515 million, with growth driven by a combination of tuition increases and enrollment growth tempered by our continued portfolio rationalization." Kramer reported "six new centers this quarter" and progress in the U.K. business, which "delivered positive operating profit for the year, a significant milestone post pandemic."
  • On ed advisory, Kramer stated "revenue increased 10% to $36 million in the quarter." He also announced several new employer clients.
  • Kramer provided 2026 guidance: "we expect revenue to be in the range of $3.075 billion to $3.125 billion and adjusted EPS to be in the range of $4.90 to $5.10 per share."
  • CFO Elizabeth Boland said, "Revenue in the fourth quarter was $734 million, representing 9% growth year-over-year and modestly ahead of our expectations." Boland added, "Adjusted operating income rose 14% to $91 million, with operating margins up roughly 60 basis points over the prior year to 12.3%. Adjusted EBITDA increased 12% to $123 million representing an adjusted EBITDA margin of 17%."

Outlook

  • Boland outlined the 2026 outlook: "we currently expect 2026 revenue to be in the range of $3.075 billion to $3.125 billion or growth of 5% to 6.5%." By segment, "full service, we expect reported revenue to grow in the range of 3.5% to 4.5% on enrollment gains and tuition increases offset by approximately 200 basis points in headwind from net center closings. In back-up care, we expect reported revenue to increase 11% to 13% and driven by the continued expansion of use. And in ed advisory, we expect to grow in the mid-single digits."
  • For 2026, "adjusted EPS...to be in the range of $4.90 to $5.10 a share." For Q1 2026, Boland stated, "our outlook is for total top line growth in the range of 6% to 7.5%" and "Q1 adjusted EPS to be in the range of $0.75 to $0.80 a share."
  • Boland also stated average price increases for 2026 are "approximately 4% and we are looking at overall enrollment for the year plus 100 basis points."

Financial Results

  • Kramer reported "revenue increased 9% to $734 million and adjusted EPS increased 17% to $1.15" in Q4 2025. For the full year, revenue was $2.93 billion and adjusted EPS $4.55.
  • Boland said "adjusted operating income rose 14% to $91 million" and "operating margins up roughly 60 basis points over the prior year to 12.3%."
  • Back-up care revenue grew 17% in Q4 to $183 million, with operating margins at 32%. Full service revenue was $515 million, up 6%, and educational advisory revenue increased 10% to $36 million.
  • Net interest expense was $12 million in Q4 2025. Cash from operations for 2025 was $351 million, capital investments $91 million, and $225 million in stock was repurchased during the year.

Q&A

  • Jeffrey Meuler, Baird: Asked about full-service margin outlook and the impact of center closures. Boland answered, "most of them are in a loss-making position, yes, because that's the reason for underperformance leading to a closure decision...overall, full service 25 to 50 bps."
  • Meuler asked about health and safety protocols and local market risks. Kramer responded, "our #1 priority continues to always be delivering high-quality care and education ... we continue to see strong retention of families in our centers."
  • Manav Patnaik, Barclays: Sought clarity on pricing and enrollment growth. Boland responded, "on average, the price increases for '26 are approximately 4% and...overall enrollment for the year plus 100 basis points."
  • Patnaik asked about New York City exposure and policy changes. Kramer replied, "we participate in UPK and that is a good relationship with the city...there is an indication that it would likely look similar to the UPK program that's in place only for younger age groups."
  • Andrew Steinerman, JPMorgan: Asked about corporate client attitudes towards increased backup benefit usage. Kramer stated, "backup is still a pretty modest line item, especially as it compares to some of the more traditional and larger benefits that they manage."
  • Keen Fai Tong, Goldman Sachs: Inquired about occupancy trajectory. Boland answered, "it would be still in the mid-60s exiting '26...growth to come though."
  • Toni Kaplan, Morgan Stanley: Requested details on center closures and drivers. Boland said, "we'd expect to be in the range of 45 to 50 or so closures this year overall."
  • Kaplan followed up on back-up care program changes. Kramer stated, "the vast, vast majority of the growth comes from the existing client base...continuing to drive users continuing to drive their frequency of use."
  • Joshua Chan, UBS: Asked about enrollment trends. Boland noted, "bit of a slowdown in the second half of the year...maybe the notable element as we're looking ahead is a little bit of an uptick in younger age group enrollment."
  • Chan also asked about net center openings. Boland responded, "in '26, we'd look to be opening plus/minus 20 or so. And...closing 45 to 50."
  • Stephanie Moore, Jefferies: Sought a view on pricing trends and optimal enrollment. Boland replied, "measured approach to tuition increases that tries to balance the economics, covering costs...our target would still be to aim for 70%."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts focused on sustainability of margin improvements, risk from center closures, pricing strategy, and the durability of backup care growth, showing a neutral to slightly positive tone but pressing for clarity around portfolio changes and forward guidance.
  • Management maintained a confident tone in prepared remarks, stating, "we are optimistic about the opportunities in front of us and look to build on the momentum we saw in 2025," and in Q&A, used phrases like "we feel confident in our position" and "we are heartened by the continued interest."
  • Compared to Q3, the analyst tone stayed consistent, with continued scrutiny on enrollment, center rationalization, and the sustainability of growth. Management’s tone remained positive but was more explicit about the pace and rationale of closures and the trajectory for occupancy.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance for 2026 revenue and EPS was more specific, with a narrower growth range and explicit mention of headwinds from center closures.
  • Strategic focus shifted further toward portfolio rationalization, with a higher number of expected closures (45-50 in 2026 vs. 25-30 previously estimated for 2026), reflecting an acceleration in addressing underperforming centers.
  • Analysts continued to focus on occupancy, margin sustainability, and backup care growth, while management was more transparent about the gradual path to higher occupancy and the ongoing impact from closures.
  • Key metric changes included slightly lower projected enrollment growth and a reinforced expectation for steady price increases around 4%.
  • Management confidence remained high, though more measured in discussing the pace of improvement and headwinds.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management identified ongoing portfolio rationalization as a challenge, noting that closures involve "some tail to those costs even as the center ceases operations if we're running dark and/or we're not able to fully exit the lease."
  • There was acknowledgment of regional enrollment variability and some centers with persistent underperformance.
  • Analysts raised concerns about policy changes in New York City and the sustainability of backup care benefit expansion, but management reported continued strong client and family retention and positive relationships with regulators.
  • The company faces ongoing market headwinds in certain geographies and a gradual path to increasing occupancy above the mid-60% range.

Final Takeaway

Bright Horizons closed 2025 with strong financial results and outperformance in both revenue and adjusted EPS, driven by robust backup care growth and steady gains in full service and educational advisory. Looking to 2026, management projects continued top-line and earnings growth, underpinned by disciplined portfolio rationalization, measured tuition increases, and expanded penetration within existing client bases. The company remains focused on driving operational efficiencies, addressing underperforming centers, and investing in core segments to support sustainable long-term growth.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Expedia outlines 2026 revenue growth of 6%–9% with margin expansion as AI and B2B drive momentum
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Expedia Group, Inc. (EXPE) Q4 2025

Management View

  • CEO Ariane Gorin reported, "We accelerated both bookings and revenue growth and expanded margins by over 2 points. We returned Vrbo and Hotels.com to growth while sustaining the performance of Brand Expedia, B2B and advertising." Gorin emphasized, "In the fourth quarter, we exceeded our expectations, growing bookings and revenue by 11% and expanding our margins by 4 points." Booked room nights increased 9%, with double-digit gains outside the U.S., and B2B bookings rose 24% while advertising revenue climbed 19%.
  • Gorin highlighted, "Our sites and apps are 30% faster than they were a year ago." She noted upgrades to the checkout path, new payment options, and greater AI integration in product personalization. The company expanded VrboCare and improved customer service tools, resulting in "record traveler self-service levels."
  • On supply, Gorin indicated the lodging property count grew by more than 10% year-over-year. Promotional rates made up over 30% of Q4 bookings, rising more than 10 points sequentially. Nearly 70% more properties participated in the Black Friday sale.
  • B2B growth was described as "fantastic," with double-digit regional growth, new partnerships, and more active agents than any prior Q4. Expedia launched a Cancel for Any Reason product and announced its intent to acquire Tiqets to broaden activity offerings.
  • Gorin commented on advertising, "We reaccelerated revenue growth and finished the year with a record number of active partners." The company introduced video ads on Expedia’s homepage and emphasized growing relevance through AI tools.
  • Gorin stressed direct engagement, stating, "2/3 of our bookings come from travelers who begin their planning journey directly with our brands, and those direct bookings are growing faster than bookings from indirect channels."
  • Operating efficiencies and margin expansion were credited to "continued operational discipline and volume leverage," with particular success in B2C marketing spend reallocation and AI-driven internal improvements.
  • CFO Scott Schenkel stated, "We grew share in the U.S. for both hotel and Vrbo and held lodging share globally. We also saw continued strength from B2B, which was a meaningful driver to our overall performance in the quarter."

Outlook

  • Schenkel provided guidance: "For the first quarter, we expect gross bookings growth to be between 10% to 12% with revenue of 11% to 13%." The company expects FX tailwinds to add approximately 3 points to bookings and 4 points to revenue. EBITDA margins are projected to increase by 3 to 4 points in Q1.
  • For full year 2026, Expedia expects gross bookings growth of 6% to 8% and revenue growth of 6% to 9%, including 1 and 2 points of FX tailwind, respectively. Full-year margins are forecast to expand by 100 to 125 basis points.
  • The company anticipates a more moderate pace of margin expansion in 2026, as it laps benefits from previous headcount and marketing optimization.

Financial Results

  • Gross bookings and revenue each increased 11% to $27 billion and $3.5 billion, respectively. B2C gross bookings were $18.3 billion, up 5%, with B2C revenue at $2.2 billion, up 4%. B2C EBITDA margins reached 31.5%, up 6 points year-over-year.
  • B2B gross bookings grew 24% to $8.7 billion; B2B revenue also rose 24% to $1.3 billion, with EBITDA margins at 24%, down 1 point.
  • Adjusted EBITDA for Q4 was $848 million with a margin of 24%. Adjusted EPS was $3.78, up 58%. Free cash flow for the year was $3.1 billion. Expedia ended the quarter with $5.7 billion in unrestricted cash and short-term investments.
  • The quarterly dividend was raised by 20% to $0.48 per share, and the company repurchased 1.1 million shares in Q4.

Q&A

  • Mark Stephen Mahaney, Evercore ISI: Asked about product enhancements for travel planning and marketing leverage. Gorin responded that improvements begin with targeted marketing and move to conversion tools, especially natural language AI and point solutions. Schenkel stated, "We've leveraged about 50 basis points as a percentage of GBV in B2C."
  • Eric Sheridan, Goldman Sachs: Inquired about brand positioning. Gorin explained, "Positioning Expedia as the one-stop shop... Hotels.com as the hotel pure play... For Vrbo, positioning it as the trusted pure-play vacation rental marketplace."
  • Jed Kelly, Oppenheimer: Asked about margin trajectory. Gorin indicated ongoing potential for margin expansion, citing B2C, B2B, new lines of business, supply growth, and ads as drivers. Schenkel noted, "We've taken a number of actions... on headcount, but also on marketing costs, on cloud costs."
  • Conor Cunningham, Melius Research: Queried about supply growth and AI's impact. Gorin noted property onboarding is "70% faster than it was before," expecting continued supply growth and integration with large language models.
  • Jacob Seed, TD Cowen: Asked about Google traffic and B2B marketing costs. Gorin reported no material changes and active experimentation with partners like Google. Schenkel clarified B2B expenses are aligned with revenue and more commission-driven.
  • Kenneth Gawrelski, Wells Fargo: Focused on B2B margin pressure. Schenkel explained that reinvestment in B2B would weigh on near-term margins but drive long-term growth.
  • Deepak Mathivanan, Cantor Fitzgerald: Asked about AI product development. Gorin described simultaneous advancement in personalization and natural language interfaces, with AI usage grounded in Expedia’s proprietary data.
  • Naved Khan, B. Riley Securities: Asked about alternative lodging and CapEx. Gorin said alternative lodging is growing but not at maximum potential. Schenkel projected CapEx to be roughly in line with 2025.
  • Lee Horowitz, Deutsche Bank: Inquired about B2C acceleration and loyalty. Gorin emphasized building trust and proprietary reviews, while Schenkel declined to provide business unit guidance but expects stable growth rates across units.
  • Trevor Young, Barclays: Asked about supply growth and Tiqets. Gorin stated supply benefits both B2B and B2C, with Tiqets expertise expected to help both segments.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts pressed on competitive positioning, product innovation, margin sustainability, and the impact of AI—tone was generally neutral, with some seeking more detail or expressing cautious optimism.
  • Management delivered confident and measured responses, frequently referencing operational discipline, data-driven decision-making, and ongoing investments. Gorin’s language remained optimistic, with recurring emphasis on growth potential and strategic execution. Schenkel offered pragmatic views on cost controls and margin outlook.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, both analysts and management maintained a steady, constructive tone, with management showing slightly increased confidence due to continued momentum and execution success.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Management raised full-year guidance in Q3, then reported Q4 results that exceeded expectations, leading to a dividend increase and continued share repurchases.
  • Strategic focus evolved from accelerating platform benefits and brand repositioning in Q3 to operational efficiency, supply growth, and AI-powered personalization in Q4.
  • Analysts’ questions in both quarters centered on B2B growth, marketing leverage, AI-driven product innovation, and margin expansion, but Q4 saw more attention to supply growth, direct channel share, and the impact of emerging AI technologies.
  • Key metrics such as booked room nights, B2B bookings, and margin expansion continued to improve, with explicit focus on maintaining growth while investing in new capabilities.
  • Management’s confidence in execution and long-term value creation strengthened, as reflected in increased guidance and capital return initiatives.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management cited macro uncertainty and geopolitical issues in Asia as factors weighing on growth in certain regions.
  • There was acknowledgment of competitive pressures from hotels seeking more direct bookings, as well as the evolving landscape of AI-powered travel planning.
  • Management is mitigating risks by investing in AI, enhancing customer support, and optimizing costs, especially in cloud and marketing spend.
  • Analysts raised concerns about B2B margin pressure due to reinvestment, the pace of supply growth, and the impact of AI-driven changes in search and distribution channels.

Final Takeaway

Expedia Group closed 2025 with strong momentum, exceeding expectations on both bookings and revenue growth while expanding margins. With a clear focus on AI-driven personalization, supply expansion, and disciplined cost management, the company expects to deliver 6% to 9% revenue growth and further margin expansion in 2026. Investments in B2B, advertising, and direct channel engagement underscore management’s confidence in sustaining profitable growth and creating long-term value for shareholders.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: NorthWestern outlines $3.21B capital plan and 5% EPS growth target as merger and data center deals advance
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: NorthWestern Energy Group, Inc. (NWE) Q4 2025

Management View

  • CEO Brian Bird highlighted major strategic achievements, including the announcement of an all-stock Merger of Equals with Black Hills Corporation, closing of acquisitions for Avista and Puget Colstrip interests as of January 1, 2026, and the inclusion of a $300 million, 131-megawatt South Dakota natural gas project in the company’s ongoing capital plan. Bird also noted the acquisition of Energy West and Cut Bank Gas natural gas distribution assets.
  • Bird stated, "On the legislative front, Montana Senate Bill 301 was signed in the law, providing greater confidence for transmission investment in Montana. And Montana House Bill 490 signed into law, which clarifies and limits wildfire-related risks, protecting our customers, communities and investors."
  • Bird reported, "During the year, we signed our third letter of intent with Quantica for 500-plus megawatts data center. And we progressed with Sabey from a letter of intent to a development agreement."
  • CFO Crystal Lail reported, "We delivered GAAP earnings of $2.94, which included impacts of merger-related costs, the regulatory outcome rate case in Montana and a very warm fourth quarter... Adjusting for those items, as I mentioned, we delivered $3.58, and that's the efforts after quite a few headwinds during the year to deliver upon our commitments to our shareholders."

Outlook

  • The company is initiating 2026 earnings guidance in the range of $3.68 to $3.83 per share, representing 5% growth at the midpoint off of 2025 results. Lail explained, "A significant part of that is... updating our capital plan. Brian mentioned the inclusion of the 131-megawatt generating facility in South Dakota. And also, we've updated to include our incremental Colstrip ownership."
  • The 5-year capital plan is updated to $3.21 billion, a 17% increase over the prior plan. The base capital plan remains self-funded, but the incremental South Dakota generation investment will require equity beyond 2026, with a 50-50 debt-to-equity approach.

Financial Results

  • GAAP diluted EPS for the full year was $2.94, and non-GAAP diluted EPS was $3.58, with adjusted fourth quarter earnings of $1.17. Dividend is increased by 1.5% to $0.67 per share. Lail noted, "Our improved margin reflects new rates, a lot of regulatory execution involved in getting to those numbers, which were offset a bit by mild weather... and impact of market prices in our Montana PCCAM mechanism."
  • The quarter included a one-time charge for the Montana rate review outcome related to the Yellowstone County Generating Station ($0.38) and merger costs ($0.03), partially offset by a $0.12 tax benefit.
  • The company closed 2025 at a lower FFO to debt, attributed to mild weather affecting cash flows and undercollection of supply costs on the Montana side, but management remains committed to credit quality.

Q&A

  • Shahriar Pourreza, Wells Fargo: Asked about the timing and scope of the large-load tariff filing for data centers. Lail responded, "We expect to file that once we have a signed ESA so that we can walk through the specific mechanics with the Montana Commission... we will plan to file that large load tariff in sync with that."
  • Pourreza also inquired about the merger and stakeholder education regarding data center cost causation. Bird replied, "We utilities certainly have been supportive of that effort. And I think what we need to demonstrate... is from a tariff perspective, and that's our plan..."
  • Aidan Kelly, JPMorgan: Questioned delays in executing ESAs for data centers and the impact on growth outlook. Bird acknowledged, "I think we -- thinking at ESAs, we at times were the holdup... unfortunately, for Sabey, they ran into this land issue... I'm also very confident in terms of where we sit with these 3 providers today."
  • Nicholas Campanella, Bank of America: Sought clarification on ESA strategy and its impact on equity needs. Lail stated, "We've said repeatedly that we size our base capital plan based off our cash flow availability and to hit our credit quality metrics... For stuff that drives growth as anything large load would, that's the type of stuff we'd look to maintain equity issuances where that makes sense."
  • Paul Fremont, Ladenburg: Asked about the South Dakota plant turbine queue and Colstrip environmental upgrades. Bird confirmed, "We're already making an investment in '26 for turbines... the plant is expected to be completed in 2030."
  • Rex Savage, Clear Street: Inquired about state regulatory processes and merger review timing. Lail said, "We are working with staff on resetting a bit of an extension to that procedural schedule. I don't have any concerns there."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts focused on the timing and certainty of data center agreements, merger regulatory processes, and financing strategies, with a generally neutral to slightly cautious tone as they pressed for specifics on execution, delays, and impacts on credit quality.
  • Management expressed confidence in strategic progress and regulatory outcomes, acknowledging challenges such as weather and regulatory headwinds, but maintaining a constructive tone—"We are pleased on top of the mild weather that I talked about and the ongoing impact to our financials, we are pleased at delivering $3.58 for 2025."
  • Compared to the previous quarter, analyst inquiries moved from general strategic progress to more pointed questions about execution risks, while management’s tone remained consistently optimistic but more detailed on mitigation strategies and challenges.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • The current quarter provided new 2026 earnings guidance ($3.68 to $3.83), increased the capital plan by 17%, and finalized the acquisition of additional Colstrip interests. The previous quarter maintained guidance and flagged pending updates for Q4.
  • Strategic focus shifted from integrating acquisitions and merger filings to progressing data center negotiations and advancing the South Dakota gas project.
  • Analysts in Q4 were more focused on near-term execution risks (data center ESAs, capital plan funding) compared to broader strategic questions in Q3.
  • Both quarters highlighted merger progress, but Q4 contained more detail on regulatory and legislative developments supporting future investment and risk management.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management cited mild weather and undercollection of Montana supply costs as key financial headwinds impacting cash flow and credit metrics.
  • The Montana rate review resulted in a one-time charge and a disallowance related to Yellowstone County Generating Station, with management seeking reconsideration but without a clear timeline.
  • Data center deal execution and associated tariff filings remain contingent on finalizing ESAs, introducing timing uncertainty.
  • Potential environmental regulations around Colstrip could require future investment, but no material environmental CapEx is currently included in the 5-year plan.
  • State regulatory timelines for the merger are being extended but are not seen by management as a cause for concern.

Final Takeaway

NorthWestern Energy Group closed 2025 with significant strategic progress, including advancing its merger with Black Hills, updating its capital plan to $3.21 billion, and targeting 5% EPS growth for 2026. Despite headwinds from mild weather and regulatory outcomes, management remains confident in its ability to deliver shareholder value through disciplined capital planning, regulatory execution, and careful management of large-load customer opportunities. The company expects further clarity on data center agreements and merger approvals in 2026, with a continued focus on reliability, affordability, and shareholder returns.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Nabors Industries outlines 6%–8% normalized EBITDA growth for 2026 while expanding international rig count and reducing debt
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Nabors Industries Ltd. (NBR) Q4 2025

Management View

  • Anthony Petrello, Chairman, President & CEO, highlighted the completion of several significant transactions: "During 2025 and through the beginning of this year, we completed a sequence of significant transactions, beginning with the purchase of Parker Wellbore for Nabors shares and assumption of debt, followed by the sale of Quail Tools and finishing with debt redemptions and a significant debt refinancing." Petrello stated the company reduced net debt by $554 million, resulting in a lower annualized cash interest expense by approximately $45 million and a portfolio from Parker expected to contribute at least $70 million in adjusted EBITDA for 2026.
  • Petrello emphasized stronger performance in the U.S. Drilling segment and continued momentum in international growth, particularly in the Lower 48 and Middle East. The PACE-X Ultra rig was noted as a key technology driver: "The first unit has been working for Catalyst in South Texas since mid-September. We had high expectations for this rig. It has delivered."
  • Petrello added, "Our net debt is down by more than $550 million. It stands at the lowest level since 2005."
  • Miguel Rodriguez, Chief Financial Officer, stated, "Full year 2025 revenue was $3.2 billion, reflecting growth of 8.7% year-over-year, driven primarily by the acquisition of Parker and a strong international expansion. Full year adjusted EBITDA was $913 million, $31 million higher than the prior year."
  • Rodriguez reported, "Fourth quarter consolidated revenue was $798 million, a decrease of $21 million or 2.5% sequentially," noting the impact from the Quail Tools divestiture, but also highlighted sequential growth excluding Quail. He added, "EBITDA was $222 million, representing an EBITDA margin of 27.8%, down 110 basis points sequentially."

Outlook

  • Rodriguez outlined that for Q1 2026, U.S. Drilling average rig count is expected to reach 64 to 65 rigs, with daily adjusted gross margin around $13,200. For international drilling, average rig count is projected at 91 to 92 rigs, with average daily gross margin in the $17,500 to $17,600 range.
  • For full year 2026, Nabors expects EBITDA growth of 6% to 8% normalized for Quail, maintaining the same EBITDA level as reported in 2025. Rodriguez also stated, "For international drilling, we expect average rig count of 96 to 98 rigs with a December exit at or above 101 rigs."
  • Nabors Drilling Solutions EBITDA is expected to grow by 6% to 7% normalized for Quail, reaching $160 million to $170 million, largely led by strong growth in international markets.
  • Rodriguez commented, "With these funds and some cash in hand, we plan to further reduce Nabors gross debt by at least $100 million during the year."

Financial Results

  • Petrello reported adjusted EBITDA for Q4 totaled $222 million, surpassing previous guidance, and cited improved performance in the U.S. Drilling segment and the legacy Drilling Solutions segment, excluding Quail.
  • Rodriguez broke down international drilling revenue for Q4 at $424 million, with an EBITDA of $131 million and margin of 31%. U.S. Drilling revenue was $241 million, EBITDA $93 million, and margin 38.7%.
  • Average daily margin in the Lower 48 increased by $152 to $13,303, with rig count ramping to 66 post-quarter end.
  • Rig Technologies revenue for Q4 was $38 million and EBITDA was $5 million, linked to year-end equipment sales.
  • Capital expenditures for Q4 were $158 million; for 2025, $695 million, including $274 million for SANAD newbuilds. Adjusted free cash flow for Q4 was $132 million, and for the year, approximately $117 million.

Q&A

  • Derek Podhaizer, Piper Sandler: Asked about drivers behind the increasing Lower 48 rig count and customer mix. Petrello replied that Nabors is now "80% public and our gas rig count is 20%, which is double from where we were before," highlighting the trend towards longer laterals and technology deployment like the PACE-X Ultra.
  • Keith MacKey, RBC: Asked about SANAD rig activations and labor market tightness in Saudi Arabia. Petrello responded, "We're highly confident those 2 rigs are going on the schedule I just outlined, which is the second and third quarter. There's no question about that from our point of view."
  • Scott Gruber, Citigroup: Queried Mexico rig redeployments and SANAD CapEx. Rodriguez clarified, "The plan for the year originally was to be around $360 million. We are at the $274 million mark in 2025, which means... probably around the $85 million that is moving from one year to another."
  • John Daniel, Daniel Energy Partners: Asked about second half caution and new can rig wrenches. Petrello replied, "It's more just the constant -- all the external noise, the EIA, even as of last week, is talking about oversupply and the market's reaction... we have everybody really focused on the cost structure here to plan if the downside does occur."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts raised questions on rig count growth, SANAD program execution, and free cash flow inflection, demonstrating a neutral to cautiously optimistic sentiment. There was a focus on CapEx discipline and execution risks but no strongly negative tone.
  • Management maintained a confident and constructive tone in both prepared remarks and Q&A, especially regarding debt reduction, technology deployment, and international growth. Petrello expressed confidence: "We remain pretty bullish on the long-term picture for gas, and we think that all our strengths play to that as well."
  • Compared to the previous quarter, management appeared more upbeat on international opportunities and debt reduction, while analysts remained consistent in focus on operational execution and financial discipline.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance language shifted from cautious optimism in Q3 to more explicit targets for EBITDA growth and rig count expansion in Q4.
  • Strategic focus increased on international rig deployments and technology upgrades, with the PACE-X Ultra and automation highlighted as differentiators.
  • Analysts continued to probe on free cash flow, SANAD project timing, and cost absorption, mirroring Q3 priorities.
  • Management’s tone grew more confident, especially with visible progress on debt reduction and improved capital structure. Analyst tone remained largely unchanged, with an emphasis on financial metrics and near-term execution.
  • Key metric changes include increased sequential EBITDA (excluding Quail), greater international rig count growth, higher Lower 48 rig count, and improved free cash flow performance versus prior guidance.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management discussed external risks such as oil price volatility, geopolitical uncertainty, and ongoing global supply-demand imbalances.
  • Rodriguez addressed cost discipline and capital allocation, noting, "We will maintain our disciplined approach to capital investments."
  • Analysts questioned SANAD CapEx timing, free cash flow inflection, and second-half market caution, to which management reiterated focus on cost controls and operational flexibility.

Final Takeaway

Nabors Industries presented a confident outlook for 2026, targeting 6% to 8% EBITDA growth normalized for Quail and expanding its international rig count, while maintaining a disciplined approach to capital spending and prioritizing further debt reduction. Management highlighted successful execution on strategic transactions, robust technology deployment, and operational improvements, projecting continued value creation for equity holders in the coming year.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Braskem plunges after Petrobras waives rights to potential share sale
data.description:
Braskem (BAK) closed -13.2% in Thursday's trading after Petrobras (PBR) decided against exercising its first-refusal rights on Novonor's potential sale of Braskem shares, according to an SEC filing.

The decision relates to a potential transaction involving the transfer of Braskem (BAK) shares held by Novonor subsidiary NSP Investimentos S.A. to Shine I FIDC, Petrobras (PBR) said.

Braskem (BAK) and Petrobras (PBR) said additional material facts will be disclosed once final terms of the potential transaction are notified and as further developments occur.

data.title: Essential Properties outlines 2026 AFFO per share guidance growth up to 8% while capitalizing on resilient credit trends
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Essential Properties Realty Trust (EPRT) Q4 2025

Management View

  • Pete Mavoides, President, CEO & Director, highlighted "compelling earnings growth and solid returns for shareholders," noting a continued execution of a differentiated investment strategy with 85% of $296 million in Q4 investments sourced from existing relationships, an average initial cash yield of 7.7%, and a GAAP yield of 9.1%. He reported "same-store rent growth of 1.6%, strong rent coverage of 3.6x, and an improvement in our watch list," and announced an increase in 2026 AFFO per share guidance to $1.99 to $2.04, implying growth of about 7% at the midpoint and 8% at the high end. The company ended the quarter with investments in 2,300 properties and a weighted average lease term of 14 years, with only 5.2% of annual base rent expiring over the next five years.
  • A. Peil, Executive VP & Chief Investment Officer, reported portfolio credit trends remain healthy, with occupancy at 99.7% and a decline in the credit watch list to under 1%. Dispositions increased, with 19 properties sold for $48.1 million at a 6.9% yield, reducing exposure to the car wash industry to 13.7%.
  • Max Jenkins, Executive VP & COO, stated Q4 investments had a weighted average lease term of 19.4 years and annual rent escalation of 2%, with a weighted average unit-level rent coverage of 4.7x. The average investment per property was $4.6 million across 34 transactions, and the investment pipeline is supported by over $200 million in subsequent quarter activity.
  • Robert Salisbury, Executive VP, CFO, and Head of Corporate Finance & Strategy and Secretary, said, "Our AFFO per share totaled $0.49, which represents an increase of 9% versus the fourth quarter of 2024." He noted a sequential decline in G&A due to a one-time compensation reversal and declared a cash dividend of $0.31, with an AFFO payout ratio of 63%. He added, "Our retained free cash flow after dividends continues to build, reaching nearly $40 million in the fourth quarter." Pro forma net debt to annualized adjusted EBITDA RE was 3.8x at quarter end, and Salisbury reiterated the increased 2026 AFFO per share guidance.

Outlook

  • The company raised its 2026 AFFO per share guidance to a range of $1.99 to $2.04, representing approximately 7% growth at the midpoint and 8% at the high end. Management maintained investment guidance of $1 billion to $1.4 billion for 2026, with expectations of modest cap rate compression in late 2026 and a stable competitive environment. Salisbury indicated the revised guidance reflects "better-than-budgeted credit trends and a large investment pipeline, with cap rates consistent with past quarters."

Financial Results

  • The company reported Q4 GAAP net income of $68.3 million and AFFO of $99.7 million. AFFO per share was $0.49, up 9% from Q4 2024. Cash G&A for the year was $28.8 million, representing 5.1% of total revenue. Dispositions in Q4 generated $48.1 million in net proceeds. The company declared a cash dividend of $0.31 with a 63% AFFO payout ratio. Free cash flow after dividends reached nearly $40 million in Q4. Gross assets surpassed $7 billion at quarter end, and $170 million of equity was issued through the ATM program. Unsettled forward equity at quarter end totaled $332 million.

Q&A

  • Michael Goldsmith, UBS: Asked about the raised guidance range and drivers behind the change. Salisbury: The higher bottom end was attributed to better portfolio credit trends and conservative initial guidance, stating "everything had been coming in a lot better on the portfolio credit side..."
  • Goldsmith, UBS: Inquired about competition stabilization. Mavoides: "The stabilization in competition has really resulted in...a slower decrease in cap rate than we have anticipated."
  • Greg McGinniss, Scotiabank: Questioned the strong start to the year in acquisitions. Mavoides: Noted Q4 was "a little light" due to deal slippage, suggesting early-year strength is not a trend shift.
  • Caitlin Burrows, Goldman Sachs: Asked about the American Signature credit event and dividend outlook. Peil: The recovery from American Signature is expected to be normal and within historical ranges. Salisbury: "I would expect the dividend to grow, but don't have a lot of guidance for you at this point."
  • Jana Galan, BofA: Asked about cap rate compression and lease escalators. Mavoides: Expects cap rates to trend from high 7s to mid-7s, and noted "some compression in our weighted average escalations."
  • Eric Borden, BMO: Inquired about competition drivers. Mavoides: "It's really driven by the access to debt capital...the 10-year treasury rate."
  • Nicholas Joseph, Citi: Asked about ATM equity issued year-to-date. Salisbury: Approximately $10 million was issued at the start of the year.
  • Additional analyst questions focused on tenant credit, portfolio composition, and investment pipeline, with management consistently emphasizing a stable strategy and strong relationship-driven deal flow.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts maintained a neutral to slightly positive tone, focusing on guidance clarity, acquisition trends, and credit health. Questions centered on the sustainability of growth, investment pipeline, and competition, without pressing on negative issues.
  • Management’s tone was confident during prepared remarks and remained consistent in Q&A, emphasizing stability and conservative guidance. Phrases like "we feel good comfort in our tenant base" and "we have a lot of year left to play" signaled confidence, while noting ongoing conservatism in planning.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, both analysts and management sustained a constructive tone, with management more openly highlighting better credit trends as a driver for guidance.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • The bottom end of 2026 AFFO per share guidance was increased, reflecting stronger portfolio credit trends compared to the initial guidance provided in Q3. Investment guidance for 2026 remains unchanged at $1 billion to $1.4 billion. Cap rate compression expectations shifted from modest compression to a more stable outlook with only slight compression forecasted for late 2026. Portfolio rent coverage and same-store rent growth remained steady, while the watch list improved further. Analyst focus shifted from concerns about escalating competition to questions about cap rate stabilization and acquisition pace. Management’s tone was consistently confident, but with more emphasis on credit trend improvements this quarter.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management cited the potential for changes in capital markets and treasury rates to influence competition and cap rates but underscored a relationship-driven strategy as a buffer. A single credit event (American Signature) was noted, but recovery is expected within normal ranges. Analysts questioned exposure to industry concentration and the sustainability of current growth rates. Management reiterated that tenant concentration continues to decline, with the top 10 tenants comprising 16.5% of ABR and top 20 at 27.1%.

Final Takeaway

Essential Properties Realty Trust management expressed confidence in delivering sustainable earnings growth, supported by resilient credit trends, robust investment pipelines, and conservative balance sheet management. The company increased the bottom end of its 2026 AFFO per share guidance, reflecting improved credit performance and a stable competitive environment, while maintaining disciplined capital allocation and a focus on relationship-driven investments.

data.title: YPF, Eni, Abu Dhabi's XRG sign development pact to advance Argentina LNG project
data.description:
The three international owners of the proposed Argentina LNG project signed a joint development agreement Thursday, which aims to unlock Argentina's Vaca Muerta shale basin and help position the country as a long-term global liquefied natural gas supplier.

Argentina's YPF (YPF), Italy's Eni (E), and Adnoc investment arm XRG said the agreement was a milestone for the project, ahead of an expected final investment decision in this year's H2.

Under the JDA, the partners will begin front-end engineering and design and perform other key technical, commercial and financing workstreams.

Argentina LNG is expected to deliver 12M tons/year of LNG capacity via two floating LNG facilities with a capacity of 6M tons/year each.

"This new step marks the formal inclusion of XRG into the project we have been developing together with Eni," YPF (YPF) President and CEO Horacio Marín said. "These two world-class players allow us to position Argentina LNG as one of the leading LNG projects globally."

data.title: Diebold Nixdorf targets $255M–$270M free cash flow in 2026 as operating model strengthens
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Diebold Nixdorf (DBD) Q4 2025

Management View

  • Octavio Marquez, President and CEO, highlighted 2025 as a defining year, stating Diebold Nixdorf “delivered on our commitments and most importantly, demonstrated that we are now operating a sustainable free cash flow generator with significantly more stable and predictable financial profile.” He pointed to a record $239 million in free cash flow, a doubling from the prior year, and emphasized expanded adjusted EBITDA to $485 million. Marquez noted, “We are delivering on what we said we would do quarter after quarter. This consistency is increasing predictability in our model and strengthening confidence in our long-term outlook.”
  • Marquez described growth in both banking and retail, with banking expanding into broader branch and transaction ecosystems and retail achieving three consecutive quarters of revenue growth, particularly in North America. He cited the successful pilot and transition to live store implementations of SmartVision AI solutions with a major U.S. retailer as a significant milestone.
  • The CEO underscored a record fifth consecutive quarter of positive free cash flow and two credit rating upgrades during the year. He affirmed that returning capital to shareholders remains central, referencing net leverage around 1x and ongoing share repurchases.
  • Thomas Timko, Executive VP & CFO, stated: “Q4 revenue was $1.1 billion, up 12% year-over-year and 17% sequentially, driven by growth in both product and service. In banking, high-capacity fit-for-purpose ATMs and strong performance in Europe drove results. In retail, strong point-of-sale and self-checkout performance globally drove revenue increases.”
  • Timko explained, “Q4 adjusted EBITDA reached $164 million, up 46% year-over-year with 350 basis points of margin expansion. Sequentially, we delivered 35% growth and drove an additional 200 basis points of margin improvement, bringing EBITDA margins to 14.9%.”

Outlook

  • The company introduced 2026 guidance with revenue projected between $3.86 billion and $3.94 billion, adjusted EBITDA in the range of $510 million to $535 million, and free cash flow forecast at $255 million to $270 million. Adjusted EPS guidance for 2026 is $5.25 to $5.75, assuming a 35% to 40% effective tax rate. Management pointed out, “At the midpoint, this guidance reflects approximately 22% year-over-year growth by a comparable basis when excluding certain noncash nonoperational tax benefits in 2025.”
  • The company expects product margins to remain at current high levels and service gross margin to increase up to 50 basis points year-over-year. Free cash flow is expected to remain positive in every quarter of 2026.
  • Marquez stated the company is “entering the year with momentum, a fortress balance sheet and a clear focus on delivering another year of profitable growth and cash generation.”

Financial Results

  • Q4 revenue was $1.1 billion, with total gross margin expanding to 27.1%. Operating profit for the quarter reached $129 million, while Q4 adjusted EBITDA totaled $164 million. Adjusted EPS for Q4 was $3.02 and for the full year reached $5.59, including $1.08 of noncash, nonoperational favorable items.
  • For the year, the company reported $239 million in free cash flow, more than doubling the previous year’s result of $109 million. Over $128 million was returned to shareholders via repurchases, representing around 6% of the company at an average share price of $55.47.
  • Banking segment Q4 revenue grew 11% year-over-year, with product revenue up 20% and service revenue up 5%. Retail Q4 revenue increased 12% year-over-year to over $300 million, driven by point-of-sale and self-checkout. Retail service revenue rose 8% year-over-year in Q4.
  • The company ended 2025 with over $700 million of liquidity, including $416 million in cash and an undrawn $310 million revolver, and a net debt leverage ratio at 1.1x.

Q&A

  • Matt Summerville, D.A. Davidson: Questioned first-half versus second-half revenue cadence and Q1 expectations. Marquez answered that revenue cadence should mirror 2025, with “revenues to flow very similar to 2025 with Q1 being approximately 22% of our total revenue for the year.”
  • Summerville: Asked about the impact of service investments. Marquez responded that the North America service rollout is mostly complete and that Q1 will see a slight decrease in service margins, followed by improvement from Q2 as investments yield returns, with margins expected to grow up to 50 basis points.
  • Summerville: Sought clarification on U.S. retail logo wins. Timko explained that wins were spread across grocery, pharmacy, and QSR, and the U.S. retail business is expected to grow double digits, with AI playing an important role.
  • Justin Ages, CJS Securities: Asked about further potential for improving days sales outstanding (DSO) and days inventory outstanding (DIO). Marquez indicated there is additional opportunity, expecting another 4 to 5 days improvement in DSO.
  • Ages: Inquired about capital allocation between share repurchases, tuck-ins, and refinancing the $950 million note. Marquez emphasized that share repurchases remain the priority, but the company maintains flexibility for tuck-ins, especially in services.
  • Antoine Legault, Wedbush: Asked about the opportunity ahead for banking recyclers. Timko described penetration as a “continuous cycle,” expecting to maintain shipment volume and offset margin changes in Asia through Lean initiatives.
  • Legault: Requested factors influencing EPS guidance. Marquez cited continued share buybacks and post-tax operating profit as key drivers.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analyst tone was generally positive, with questions reflecting interest in growth cadence, capital allocation, and operational improvements, but also seeking clarity on margin sustainability and investment priorities.
  • Management’s tone was confident and upbeat, frequently referencing strong execution, consistent performance, and momentum into 2026. Marquez used phrases like “entering the year with momentum” and emphasized that the company’s cash generation is “underappreciated.”
  • Compared to the previous quarter, both analysts and management continue to display confidence, with analysts showing increased focus on capital returns and operational leverage.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance language shifted from reaffirmation at the high end of ranges in Q3 to establishing higher targets for 2026, including revenue, adjusted EBITDA, and free cash flow.
  • Strategic focus has evolved from stabilizing the foundation to emphasizing growth, margin expansion, and capital allocation, particularly share repurchases.
  • Analysts remained focused on service margin recovery, retail growth, and capital allocation, while management highlighted continued operational improvements, Lean initiatives, and global expansion.
  • Financial results demonstrated acceleration in revenue, EBITDA, and free cash flow, while operating leverage and margin expansion were more prominent this quarter.
  • Management’s confidence and messaging around predictability, cash generation, and shareholder returns have strengthened compared to the prior quarter.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management cited ongoing investments in service infrastructure and technician hiring, which may temporarily impact service margins in Q1 2026 before improvement is realized.
  • There is a note on regional variability, particularly in Asia and Latin America, which may affect growth and margins, though the company expects to offset these through its Lean initiatives and diversified business model.
  • Analysts probed the limits of further working capital improvements and the balance between buybacks and strategic acquisitions, reflecting concerns about sustainable cash generation and capital allocation discipline.

Final Takeaway

Diebold Nixdorf’s management positions the company as having completed a transformational year, highlighting improved financial stability, record free cash flow, and robust growth across core banking and retail businesses. Entering 2026, the outlook features higher revenue and profit targets, continued margin expansion, and a strong commitment to capital returns, with management reiterating confidence in the durability of its operating model and ongoing ability to deliver shareholder value.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Karyopharm signals $130M–$150M revenue target for 2026 with pivotal myelofibrosis data readout ahead
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Karyopharm Therapeutics Inc. (KPTI) Q4 2025

Management View

  • Richard Paulson, President and CEO, highlighted Karyopharm entering a “defining phase, marked by important late-stage clinical milestones, continued disciplined execution and the opportunity to meaningfully expand the impact and scale of our oncology franchise.” He indicated selinexor’s “durable commercial foundation in multiple myeloma” and identified myelofibrosis as the most significant near-term value driver, with endometrial cancer as a subsequent expansion opportunity.
  • Paulson stated, “We remain on track to share top line data from our Phase III SENTRY trial in March,” positioning this as a key milestone. He added, “We continue to manage the business with discipline. As previously disclosed, our cash runway extends into the second quarter, which aligns with key near-term clinical milestones.”
  • Reshma Rangwala, Executive VP, Chief Medical Officer & Head of Research, emphasized: “I’m incredibly excited by the near-term opportunity to read out two Phase III trials that could establish new standards of care in 2 areas of high unmet need.” She detailed selinexor’s mechanism and potential in myelofibrosis, referencing “a substantial body of preclinical, nonclinical translational and clinical efficacy as well as safety data sets.”
  • Sohanya Cheng, Chief Commercial Officer, reported, “XPOVIO net product revenue grew to $32.1 million in the fourth quarter of 2025 and $114.9 million for full year 2025. We expect to continue to deliver revenue growth this year and are guiding towards $115 million to $130 million of XPOVIO net product revenue in 2026.”
  • Lori Macomber, CFO, stated, “Total revenue for the fourth quarter of 2025 was $34.1 million, an increase of 11.8% compared to the fourth quarter of 2024. For the year, total revenue was $146.1 million, a slight increase from 2024.” She also noted, “Our loss from operations improved by approximately 43% in the fourth quarter of 2025 compared to the fourth quarter of 2024.”

Outlook

  • Macomber provided 2026 financial guidance: “Total revenue of $130 million to $150 million, consisting of U.S. XPOVIO net product revenue and license royalty and milestone revenue expected to be earned from our partners, primarily Menarini and Antengene. U.S. XPOVIO net product revenue to be in the range of $115 million to $130 million. R&D and SG&A expenses to be in the range of $230 million to $245 million.”
  • Paulson described the company as “well positioned as we approach pivotal data that will inform the next phase of the company,” and reaffirmed commitment to “managing the business with a clear focus on near-term value-creating milestones.”
  • There was no explicit change to guidance language compared to the previous quarter, but the focus is now squarely on the upcoming March data readout for myelofibrosis.

Financial Results

  • XPOVIO net product revenue was $32.1 million in Q4 2025 and $114.9 million for the full year.
  • Total revenue for Q4 2025 was $34.1 million, and $146.1 million for the year.
  • Research and development expenses for Q4 were $27.7 million, down 17% year-over-year; full year R&D expenses were $125.6 million, down 12%.
  • Selling, general and administrative expenses for Q4 were $22.8 million, down 16% year-over-year; full year SG&A was $105.2 million, down 9%.
  • Net loss was $102.2 million or $5.71 per share in Q4, and $196 million or $17.93 per diluted share for the full year, with Macomber explaining, “More than half of the full year loss was driven by below-the-line items, including the loss on extinguishment of debt and interest expense, which are largely noncash in nature.”
  • Cash, cash equivalents, restricted cash and investments at year-end were $64.1 million.

Q&A

  • Colleen Hanley, Robert W. Baird & Co.: Asked about differences between 40 mg and 60 mg dosing in Phase I. Rangwala responded, “When you look at the efficacy and safety, there's a clear benefit risk in favor of the 60-milligram cohort as compared to the 40 mg, largely due to the fact that efficacy, both from an SVR as well as TSS was maximized in the 60-milligram group as compared to the 40-milligram group.”
  • Hanley asked about Novartis’s moves in myelofibrosis. Paulson answered, “Given our top line readout data is going to happen in March, we would look to be filing rapidly with our data, obviously engaging in both the U.S. and globally.”
  • Ioannis Souroutzidis, Cantor Fitzgerald: Inquired about blinded safety data and dose modifications. Rangwala replied, “No, we haven't updated anything beyond what we've publicly disclosed in the past,” and added that dose modifications for selinexor and ruxolitinib were per protocol and consistent with label guidance.
  • Brian Abrahams, RBC Capital Markets: Questioned patient population and efficacy expectations. Rangwala stated, “Yes, this potentially could be a less sick patient population. With that said, though, I don't think it's going to have any impact on the efficacy.”
  • Jonathan Chang, Leerink Partners: Asked about confidence in hitting the TSS endpoint. Rangwala cited “multiple different aspects that give us confidence,” including strong Phase I data, cytokine reductions, and the FDA’s acceptance of the more sensitive absolute TSS endpoint.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts were optimistic but focused on clarifying trial details, dosing rationale, and competitive positioning. Their tone was engaged and constructive, with no overt skepticism or negativity.
  • Management maintained a confident and energized tone throughout, particularly regarding near-term clinical milestones and the commercial outlook. Paulson described the organization as “energized and well positioned to deliver on this opportunity.”
  • The tone from both analysts and management is largely consistent with the previous quarter, but the urgency and anticipation have increased as pivotal data approaches.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • The strategic focus has shifted from trial enrollment and financial restructuring to imminent clinical data readouts and commercial execution for myelofibrosis.
  • Financial discipline and cost control remain a theme, with further reductions in R&D and SG&A expenses.
  • Guidance for 2026 is in line with prior outlook, but now incorporates the anticipated impact of upcoming clinical milestones.
  • Analysts’ questions have become more technically focused on trial design, data interpretation, and competitive positioning versus prior quarter’s broader commercial strategy queries.
  • Management’s confidence and anticipation are more pronounced as pivotal results near, compared to the more methodical tone in the previous quarter.

Risks and Concerns

  • Macomber reminded that cash runway “extends into the second quarter, which aligns with key near-term clinical milestones,” highlighting the need for additional financing depending on trial outcomes.
  • Paulson acknowledged “actively evaluating a range of financing and strategic options to maintain flexibility and align capital decisions with value creation.”
  • The end of R&D reimbursement from Menarini in 2025 was stated, impacting future non-product revenue streams.
  • No new safety or trial discontinuation concerns were raised; management reiterated protocol adherence and confidence in trial design.

Final Takeaway

Karyopharm management underscores a pivotal inflection point in 2026, with the Phase III SENTRY trial in myelofibrosis set for a March data readout, and top line results from the XPORT-EC-042 endometrial cancer trial expected mid-year. The company reaffirms disciplined cost management and financial guidance of $130 million to $150 million in total revenue for 2026, highlighting a durable commercial foundation and readiness for potential expansion pending positive trial outcomes. Management remains focused on executing near-term value-creating milestones and maintaining operational flexibility as Karyopharm advances its late-stage oncology pipeline.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript
